decisions.rationale: Both mentions refer to the gradient of a scalar-valued function, a fundamental concept in multivariable calculus. Evidence_001 defines the gradient as a vector of partial derivatives, and evidence_008 expresses the gradient of a composite function using the chain rule. The concept is identical across lectures.

evidence_spans.span: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) be a differentiable scalar-valued function. A partial derivative measures how \(f\) changes when one coordinate changes while the other coordinates are held fixed. The gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) collects these partial derivatives into a vector. For a unit direction \(u\), the directional derivative is \(\nabla f(x)\cdot u\), so the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase. A point where the gradient is the zero vector is called a stationary point.

decisions.rationale: Both mentions refer to the gradient of a differentiable function, defined as a vector of partial derivatives, used in calculus and optimization contexts. Evidence shows identical mathematical definition and notation.

evidence_spans.span: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) be a differentiable scalar-valued function. A partial derivative measures how \(f\) changes when one coordinate changes while the other coordinates are held fixed. The gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) collects these partial derivatives into a vector. For a unit direction \(u\), the directional derivative is \(\nabla f(x)\cdot u\), so the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase. A point where the gradient is the zero vector is called a stationary point.

If \(f\) is differentiable, the gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) describes local change. The negative gradient \(-\nabla f(x)\) is a descent direction because it points toward the direction of steepest local decrease. Gradient descent is a first-order iterative method that repeatedly moves in this direction:

decisions.rationale: Hessian Matrix is a specific matrix of second-order partial derivatives used in calculus to describe curvature, while Matrix in linear algebra is a general rectangular array representing linear transformations. They are different concepts at different granularities and contexts.

evidence_spans.span: For twice differentiable functions, a second-order approximation also includes the Hessian matrix, which describes local curvature. These approximations are useful because they turn a complicated function into a local linear or quadratic model. In optimization, this local model helps explain why the gradient gives a useful direction for changing the input.

A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers that can represent a linear transformation once bases have been chosen for the input and output spaces. Matrix multiplication corresponds to composing linear transformations, and changing a basis changes the matrix representation without changing the underlying transformation.

decisions.rationale: Both mentions define 'Stationary Point' as a point where the gradient is zero, and the type is Concept in both lectures. Evidence_001 from calculus_001 and evidence_009 from optimisation_001 provide identical definitions, confirming the same educational object at the same granularity.

evidence_spans.span: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}\) be a differentiable scalar-valued function. A partial derivative measures how \(f\) changes when one coordinate changes while the other coordinates are held fixed. The gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) collects these partial derivatives into a vector. For a unit direction \(u\), the directional derivative is \(\nabla f(x)\cdot u\), so the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase. A point where the gradient is the zero vector is called a stationary point.

For convex functions, every local minimum is also a global minimum. A stationary point is a point where the gradient is zero. In smooth optimisation, stationary points are important because they describe candidates for local optima.

decisions.rationale: Jacobian Matrix is a specific matrix of partial derivatives from calculus, while Matrix in linear algebra is a general rectangular array representing linear transformations. They are different concepts at different granularities and contexts.

evidence_spans.span: For a differentiable vector-valued function \(g\), the Jacobian matrix \(J_g(x)\) collects all first-order partial derivatives of the output components of \(g\). It gives the best local linear approximation to \(g\) near \(x\).

A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers that can represent a linear transformation once bases have been chosen for the input and output spaces. Matrix multiplication corresponds to composing linear transformations, and changing a basis changes the matrix representation without changing the underlying transformation.
